1.synthesis:
The Philistines muster for war against Israel; Achish tells David he will march with the Philistines, and David gives an ambiguous answer: “Very well, you shall know what your servant can do” (v. 2). Achish interprets this as commitment: I will make you my bodyguard forever. David is now committed — or appears committed — to fighting against Israel in the battle that will kill Saul and Jonathan. The narrative pivots sharply to Saul in v. 3: Samuel is dead (a reminder); Saul had expelled the mediums and necromancers from the land; and the Philistine army, seeing it, terrifies Saul, who inquires of YHWH but receives no answer — no dreams, no Urim, no prophets (v. 6).

The three channels of YHWH’s communication — dreams, Urim, prophets — are all closed to Saul. The Urim belongs to Abiathar, who is with David; the prophets will not speak to him; dreams yield nothing. Saul’s silence from YHWH is the accumulated consequence of a reign in which he has repeatedly disobeyed, driven away, and attempted to kill the very instruments of YHWH’s communication. The prophet is dead; the priest is with his enemy; the dreams give no answer. Saul is alone with his fear and the silence of the God he has spent his reign disappointing.
1.historical_context: The expulsion of mediums and necromancers (practitioners of ob and yiddeoni) from Israel was commanded in Leviticus 19:31 and Deuteronomy 18:10–11 — one of Saul’s few acts of covenant faithfulness in his later career. The irony is that he is about to violate this very command in desperation. The Urim, which enabled priestly oracular inquiry, was with Abiathar (23:6) — Saul’s own massacre of Nob had destroyed his access to legitimate priestly inquiry. Saul’s communication channels to YHWH are closed by his own prior actions, layer by layer.
1.literary_structure: 28:1–6 is a tight narrative hinge: it interweaves David’s dangerous Philistine commitment with Saul’s desperate divine silence. The two threads — where is David? what will happen at the battle? — are held in tension throughout the chapter. Saul’s attempt to inquire of YHWH and receive nothing is the antitype of David’s consistent inquiry and direction through Abiathar. The structural contrast is complete: David has YHWH’s priestly access; Saul has YHWH’s silence. Both are facing the same battle; their different positions before YHWH produce different information and different outcomes.
1.theological_application: Saul’s silence from YHWH is not arbitrary punishment but the consequence of choices that have closed channel after channel of divine communication. He expelled the mediums (covenant faithfulness), then drove away the priests (covenant violation), and now faces the battle without any remaining means of knowing YHWH’s will. The principle is not that YHWH plays favorites but that relationship with YHWH is maintained through the practices and personnel that sustain it — and when those have been systematically destroyed, the silence that follows is the sound of a relationship the human partner has effectively terminated. Communities of faith that dismantle the structures of prophetic witness, priestly service, and honest prayer in favor of royal convenience will eventually face Saul’s silence.
1.christology: Saul’s three channels of divine inquiry — dreams, Urim, prophets — are the same three means by which YHWH spoke to Israel throughout its history. Hebrews 1:1–2: “Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.” Where Israel’s three means are all closed to Saul in his desperate final days, the new covenant opens a single, definitive, permanent channel in the Son. Saul’s silence is the darkness before the light; the incarnation is the word that does not fail the desperate seeker. “Ask, and it will be given to you” (Matt 7:7) — the channel Saul found closed is the one Christ declared permanently open.

7.synthesis:
Saul instructs his servants to find him a medium — “seek for me a woman who is a medium, that I may go to her and inquire of her” (v. 7). He goes in disguise to En-dor, assures the medium she will not be punished, and asks her to bring up Samuel (vv. 8–11). The woman sees Samuel and cries out; she recognizes Saul by the disguise he has taken off (“Why have you deceived me? You are Saul!” — v. 12). Saul assures her she will not be punished and asks what she sees: an old man wrapped in a robe. Saul knows it is Samuel and bows (vv. 13–14).

Samuel’s words are not comfort but verdict: “Why have you disturbed me by bringing me up?” He recites the history of YHWH’s rejection and Saul’s disobedience, then delivers the final oracle: “Moreover, the LORD will give Israel also with you into the hand of the Philistines, and tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me. The LORD will give the army of Israel also into the hand of the Philistines” (v. 19). The dead prophet speaks what the living channels refused: the verdict Saul feared but could not hear through legitimate means. The spirit of Samuel does not offer hope or a path to restoration; it delivers the sentence. The seance at En-dor produces not guidance but judgment.
7.historical_context: The interpretation of what happened at En-dor has been debated throughout the history of interpretation: did Samuel actually appear, or was it a demonic impersonation, or was it the medium’s theatrical performance? The most natural reading of the text is that Samuel actually appeared — hence the medium’s genuine alarm, Saul’s immediate recognition, and the content of the oracle (which corresponds precisely to what Samuel had predicted during his life). The surprise is that YHWH permitted this occurrence; many interpreters suggest that YHWH overrode normal prohibitions to deliver a final verdict through the medium’s session without validating the practice itself.
7.literary_structure: The En-dor episode (28:7–19) is structured as the reversal of a divination session: instead of guidance, Saul receives condemnation; instead of hope, he receives a death sentence. The irony is complete — the one means of inquiry Saul had not exhausted (the medium he himself had expelled) is also closed against him, because what it produces is not the guidance he sought but the confirmation of what his other closed channels could not tell him: it is too late. The oracle is compressed and final: Israel will be given to the Philistines; tomorrow Saul and his sons will be with Samuel.
7.theological_application: The En-dor consultation is both the most desperate and the most revealing act of Saul’s final days. He violates his own command in search of guidance he cannot obtain through legitimate channels — the pattern of those who, having closed all the legitimate ways of hearing from God, turn to whatever is left. The tragedy is not that the medium produces nothing but that what she produces is judgment rather than direction: Saul is not seeking repentance or restoration; he is seeking information that might help him avoid the fate that his own choices have produced. Seeking supernatural information as an alternative to genuine repentance is the Saul-pattern at En-dor; the one thing Samuel does not offer him is a way back.
7.christology: Samuel’s word to Saul — “tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me” — is the announcement of death in the language of shared location. The dead prophet declares the dying king’s imminent arrival in the realm of the dead. Christ’s word to the dying thief on the cross — “today you will be with me in paradise” (Luke 23:43) — is the same structure: an announcement of shared location, from one who has authority to make it. But where Samuel’s word is verdict on a life of disobedience, Christ’s word is grace to one who has acknowledged his fault at the last possible moment. The same grammatical structure — “with me” — carries the weight of the entire difference between judgment and mercy.

20.synthesis:
Saul collapses — he falls immediately full length on the ground, terrified by Samuel’s words, and there is no strength in him because he has eaten nothing all day and all night (v. 20). The medium and Saul’s servants urge him to eat and he refuses (vv. 21–23). When they press him, he rises and sits on the bed while the woman prepares a meal — she has a fattened calf that she kills for him, bakes unleavened bread, and sets it before Saul and his servants, who eat. Then they rise and go away that night (vv. 24–25). Saul goes to his death.

The domestic detail of the final supper at En-dor is both humanizing and devastating: the king who will die tomorrow is fed by the woman whose practice he had outlawed, lying full length on the floor of a stranger’s house on the eve of his last battle. The fattened calf slaughtered by a medium for a king who has received his death sentence is the anti-feast of the David narrative. Samuel’s first appearance in the book was at a feast where YHWH directed him to Saul; the last appearance is in a hidden underground consultation where the dead prophet pronounces the death sentence over a king who is too terrified to eat. The trajectory from anointing feast to death-sentence supper is the arc of Saul’s entire reign.
20.historical_context: Saul’s refusal to eat until pressed reflects either ritual fasting (associated with preparation for divine encounter), emotional prostration, or both. The medium’s domestic hospitality — killing the fattened calf, baking unleavened bread — is an act of basic human care for a man she has no reason to help beyond the mercy of the moment. The meal the medium provides has sometimes been read as a dark parody of the Passover meal (unleavened bread, fattened animal) — a last supper for the rejected king before his exodus into death. The group rises and goes into the night — into the battle from which Saul will not return.
20.literary_structure: The closing of chapter 28 (28:20–25) frames Saul’s last night with deliberately domestic detail after the cosmic horror of the Samuel consultation. The collapse to the floor, the refusal to eat, the forced feeding, the rising and going away in the dark — these small actions carry the weight of the entire chapter. The chapter does not end with Saul at battle but with Saul at a table, fed by the woman who cannot save him, before the night march to Jezreel. The combination of the terrifying oracle and the ordinary meal is the narrative texture of the approach of death: life continues in its smallest details up to the moment it ends.
20.theological_application: The medium’s care for Saul in his final hour is an unexpected expression of basic human compassion toward the dying. She has no theological obligation to him and every reason to resent the risk his visit has created; yet she insists he eat, slaughters her own animal, and provides what sustenance is possible. Communities of faith can note this: the one who provides final compassion to the dying is not always the theologically expected person. Mercy at the end is rarely offered by those most confident of their own righteousness; it comes from unexpected quarters — the woman at En-dor, the thief on the adjacent cross. The final hospitality of the medium toward the condemned king is the grace of human solidarity at the edge of death.
20.christology: Saul’s last meal — provided by the medium whose practice he had outlawed, eaten on the floor of a stranger’s house the night before his death — is the inversion of every sacred meal in the OT. It contrasts with the covenant meals of Sinai, the Passover, the showbread — all of which are meals of divine presence, covenant, and life. The Last Supper, by contrast, is eaten by the one who goes into death the next day with full knowledge and full purpose: “I have eagerly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer” (Luke 22:15). Where Saul eats in prostration and terror the night before his death sentence is executed, Christ eats in deliberate covenant-making the night before he goes to fulfill it. Both last suppers precede a death; what differs is whether the death is the end of a rejected vocation or the fulfillment of the covenant purpose.
